deeperExplanation: Python lists are dynamic arrays of object pointers (`PyObject**`). CPython uses an over-allocation strategy (`0, 4, 8, 16, 25, 35, 46, 58, 72, 88...`) to ensure `.append()` achieves amortized O(1) time complexity.
internalImplementation: Inserting or removing elements at index 0 requires shifting all N pointers in memory (O(N) operation). For frequent double-ended insertion/pop, use `collections.deque` (O(1)).

examples.code:
# List In-Place Sorting vs sorted()
nums = [5, 2, 8, 1]
nums.sort(reverse=True)  # Modifies nums in-place, returns None
print('In-place:', nums) # [8, 5, 2, 1]

original = [5, 2, 8, 1]
new_list = sorted(original) # Returns brand new list
print('New list:', new_list)
examples.explanation: `.sort()` mutates list in-place returning `None`. `sorted()` leaves original untouched and returns new sorted list.

performanceConsiderations.timeComplexity: Indexing O(1), append() amortized O(1), insert(0)/pop(0) O(N), sort() O(N log N) Timsort.

debuggingWalkthrough.bugDescription: Assigning result of in-place method `.append()` to variable.
debuggingWalkthrough.incorrectCode:
items = [1, 2]
items = items.append(3)  # BUG: items becomes None!
debuggingWalkthrough.correctCode:
items = [1, 2]
items.append(3)          # Correct: mutates items in-place

bestPractices: Use `.sort()` when you don't need the original order; use `sorted()` when preserving original data.
Use `collections.deque` for O(1) queue/deque push and pop operations.
